response: Quantum computers use qubits that exploit superposition, entanglement, and interference to tackle certain problems exponentially faster than classical bit-based machines. IBM, Google, IonQ, Rigetti and others are pursuing superconducting, trapped-ion, photonic and topological hardware; Google notably claimed “quantum supremacy” in 2019 by completing a task in 200 seconds that it estimated would take a classical supercomputer 10,000 years. Yet today’s devices have only about 100–1,000 fragile, ultra-cold qubits, suffer high error rates, and will likely need millions of physical qubits plus extensive error correction before they become broadly useful. If these technical hurdles are overcome, quantum computing could transform drug discovery, materials science, cryptography, financial modeling, logistics, and other optimization problems.
